affordances.name: Allocation-backed sacrifice before recommendation
affordances.mechanism: When scarce resources make desirable options conflict, force the recommendation to name what is sacrificed, delayed, downweighted, or killed, expose the hidden cost of saying yes, and stress-test the assumptions that make the chosen trade-off acceptable.

use_when: Pursuing one objective would weaken another and the real decision is what to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.
Time, attention, money, or political capital make doing everything impossible and priorities must be ranked.
The recommendation hides opportunity cost, displaced benefit, or the actual resource reallocation behind balance language.
do_not_use_when: The situation has no material scarcity, conflicting goals, displaced benefit, or allocation decision.
The team only wants to acknowledge tension rhetorically while keeping every option and priority intact.
The problem is a simple preference list and lacks enough evidence to weigh material costs and benefits.
case_evidence_needed: The scarce resource and competing objectives that cannot all be pursued.
The option, task, customer preference, benefit, or project that would receive less time, money, attention, or priority.
The hidden opportunity cost or displaced benefit attached to saying yes.
The assumptions that must be true for the chosen sacrifice to be acceptable.

treatment_requirements.description: Translate the trade-off into a concrete allocation decision by ranking priorities and naming what gets sacrificed, delayed, downweighted, killed, or not done.
evidence_required: The objectives, benefits, or paths competing for the same scarce resource.
The time, money, attention, mental capacity, or political capital constraint.
The priority ranking or don't-do choice implied by the recommendation.
The concrete reallocation, option kill, or priority change that proves the trade-off is not rhetorical.
good_output_shape: Names the chosen priority and the desirable alternative that receives less support.
States what gets sacrificed, delayed, downweighted, killed, or not done.
Shows the opportunity cost or displaced benefit of saying yes.
Rejects balance language when no resource, option, or ranking actually changes.

treatment_requirements.description: Test whether the chosen sacrifice depends on unexamined assumptions, optimistic estimates, or a simple pro-con frame that understates costs and benefits.
evidence_required: The assumptions that must hold for the chosen trade-off to generate the expected result.
The material costs and benefits across the serious options.
The inputs or probabilities whose variation would change the recommendation.
Any legacy priority, default, or emotional attachment that could block reallocation.
good_output_shape: Asks what must be true before treating the trade-off as acceptable.
Uses systematic cost-benefit weighing when a pro-con list would be too subjective.
Identifies the assumption or input where greater accuracy is needed.
Separates real allocation discipline from motivated protection of a favored option.

diagnostic_questions: Which good thing are we choosing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight?
What hidden cost of saying yes, opportunity cost, or displaced benefit is being ignored?
What would you have to believe for this trade-off to be acceptable?
If no resources, options, or priorities change, is this only trade-off talk?
misuse_guards: Do not let trade-off language substitute for actual reallocation, option killing, or priority ranking.
Do not use a simple pro-con list when material costs and benefits need systematic weighing.
Do not let comprehensiveness theater turn the work into gathering everything after a bounded hypothesis test is enough.
Do not let hidden assumptions, defaults, or legacy priorities preserve the current allocation after new evidence.

source_evidence.source_quote: Because resources—whether time, money, mental capacity, or political capital—are scarce and expensive, an individual or organization cannot pursue all desirable paths simultaneously.
Tradeoffs force prioritization.
Strategic thinkers use heuristics to prioritize resources based on anticipated return. This includes identifying what problems to work on and, crucially, **what not to do**.
one should gather just enough facts to prove or disprove a hypothesis, recognizing that additional effort past this point is a waste of precious time and effort.
Trade-offs are strongest when the core decision is about choosing which good thing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.
The model adds value when time, attention, money, or political capital force explicit prioritization.
It is a strong fit when the main failure mode is pretending there is no downside, no opportunity cost, or no displaced benefit.
The model fails when teams acknowledge trade-offs rhetorically but refuse to actually reallocate resources, kill options, or rank priorities.
CBA is designed to analyze the benefits (pros) and costs (cons) across an array of options, allowing decision-makers to weigh options beyond mere subjective preference.
What would you have to believe?
For quantitative assessments, run analysis across inputs to uncover key drivers and appreciate where greater accuracy is needed in assumptions regarding probability.

review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed sacrifice selection, explicit resource reallocation, hidden-cost naming, and assumption stress testing into one operational affordance because the source repeatedly ties trade-offs to scarce resources, prioritization, and anti-performative allocation discipline.
dropped_material: Did not extract a generic balance affordance; the source-backed operational surface is narrower than the model name.
Did not split communication compression, Southwest positioning, management focus, and learning accuracy into separate affordances; they illustrate the same sacrifice-and-allocation mechanism.
Did not promote relation curation or structured tensions into runtime behavior.
open_questions: Review may later decide whether the bounded-evidence discipline around not boiling the ocean belongs here or in a prioritization-specific affordance.

absence_records.reason: The source is broad, but its strongest activation is restricted to scarce resources, conflicting objectives, hidden costs, and allocation changes; it does not support promoting a generic runtime habit that every decision should be framed as a trade-off.

source_evidence.source_quote: The Trade Off model is most powerful in contexts defined by constraints, conflicting goals, or complexity:
Trade-offs are strongest when the core decision is about choosing which good thing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.

absence_records.reason: The source presents compression versus comprehensiveness as a communication example of balancing detail and engagement. It does not create a separate Trade Off affordance unless the case also requires allocation-backed sacrifice, hidden-cost naming, or priority reallocation. Compression-specific behavior is better owned by abstraction, simplification, information-theory, or complexity-bias-resistance records.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Compression vs. Comprehensiveness:** In communication and explanation, the model operates as the need to find the "balance of compression and comprehensiveness".
This requires including enough essential detail and context to inform the audience while excluding enough to ensure they remain engaged and focused, rather than overwhelmed by complexity.

absence_records.reason: The source uses minmax and maxmin as a game-theory analogy for calculated trade-offs, but standalone counterparty payoff mapping belongs to game-theory-payoffs or Nash-style records. Trade-offs should only promote the allocation-backed sacrifice contract unless the case evidence requires resource reallocation, displaced benefit, or explicit priority killing.

source_evidence.source_quote: Game theorists utilize the concepts of *minmax* (choosing an outcome that maximizes the minimum possible gain) and *maxmin* (choosing an outcome that minimizes the maximum possible loss) to describe strategies where optimal trade-offs are calculated.

absence_records.reason: The source rejects trade-off talk that acknowledges tension but refuses to reallocate resources, kill options, or rank priorities. Do not promote a trade-offs card when no allocation actually changes.

source_evidence.source_quote: The model fails when teams acknowledge trade-offs rhetorically but refuse to actually reallocate resources, kill options, or rank priorities.
Trade-offs are strongest when the core decision is about choosing which good thing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.
Trade-offs are strongest when the core decision is about choosing which good thing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.
The model adds value when time, attention, money, or political capital force explicit prioritization.
It is a strong fit when the main failure mode is pretending there is no downside, no opportunity cost, or no displaced benefit.
The model fails when teams acknowledge trade-offs rhetorically but refuse to actually reallocate resources, kill options, or rank priorities.
What would you have to believe?

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the full extraction packet from /tmp, raw Markdown source, activation curation, intervention curation, relation curation, schema, and extraction contract. Extracted one high-confidence operational affordance centered on explicit sacrifice, resource reallocation, hidden-cost naming, and assumption stress testing; curation was used only as context and exact quotes come from the canonical Markdown source.
dropped_material: Did not extract generic 'all decisions have trade-offs' material despite Lane 4 frequency; the source supports narrower activation conditions.

Did not extract standalone affordances for game-theory minmax, compression versus comprehensiveness, Southwest Airlines, management focus, teaching analogies, MECE, systems thinking, Pareto, commander's intent, anchoring, availability, status-quo bias, or commitment bias.
Did not promote any runtime routing, gap-question, memo, chat, Step 6, Step 8, Lane 4, or Observatory behavior.
open_questions: A future relation-aware pass may want to preserve the status-quo-bias and commitment-bias tensions, but this record keeps them out of model affordance runtime policy.
Review may want to decide whether cost-benefit analysis should remain a treatment requirement here or be delegated entirely to a separate model record when one exists.
